key: hull|2017|putting on my best normal pmc5
bare: - Hull et al. (2017) -- "Putting on My Best Normal" (PMC5509825): Qualitative study of 92 autistic adults; camouflaging composed of assimilation, masking, and compensation (including social scripts); short- and long-term consequences include exhaustion and threats to self-perception

key: hull|2017|putting on my best normal soci
bare: Hull, L., Petrides, K. V., Allison, C., Smith, P., Baron-Cohen, S., Lai, M.-C., & Mandy, W. (2017). "Putting on my best normal": Social camouflaging in adults with autism spectrum conditions. *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 47(8), 2519-2534. [PMC5509825](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5509825/)

**Hull et al. (2017, PMC5509825)** introduced the CAT-Q (Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire) and documented that masking — the conscious modulation of communication and affect to appear less autistic — imposes substantial cognitive and emotional costs. **Hull et al. (2019)** deepened this with qualitative work on the reasons, contexts, and costs of camouflaging. **Cook et al. (2021)** conducted a systematic review that found camouflaging to be consistently associated with anxiety, depression, burnout, and the erosion of self-knowledge. [VALIDATED — one of the strongest consensus findings in contemporary autism research]

Hull, L., Mandy, W., Lai, M.-C., Baron-Cohen, S., Allison, C., Smith, P., & Petrides, K. V. (2019). Development and validation of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q). *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 49(3), 819-833. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6394785/)
urls: Hull et al. (2019) - Development and Validation of the CAT-Q

bare: - **Hull et al. (2019)** -- development of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q).
